one_line_takeaway: A non-empirical 'vision paper' argues Agent Skills' natural-language instruction carrier, single-approval persistent trust grant, and unvetted marketplace distribution structurally invert every security mitigation ChatGPT Plugins had, and organizes this claim into a 7-category/17-scenario threat taxonomy validated against 5 cited (not authors' own) real-world incidents.
key_finding: The paper's central architectural claim (Table 1; Section 3.3) is that Agent Skills simultaneously inverts every mitigating property that ChatGPT Plugins had: no data/instruction boundary, local execution at user privilege, no runtime isolation, unconstrained permission scope, and a persistent/undifferentiated/irrevocable-by-content-change trust grant from a single install-time approval -- while sharing MCP's lack of mandatory marketplace review but with a lower (near-zero) authorship barrier than MCP. All 5 incidents the authors analyze (MedusaLocker ransomware Skill, Dec 2025; ClawHavoc supply-chain campaign compromising 1,184 Skills / ~1-in-5 of the marketplace, Jan 2026; CVE-2025-59536 CVSS 8.7 and CVE-2026-21852 CVSS 5.3 config-injection/API-key-exfiltration bugs in Claude Code, patched in versions 1.0.111 and 2.0.65; SafeDep's PEP 723 deferred-dependency demonstration; Mitiga's silent codebase-exfiltration demonstration in 4 user interactions with an empty skill-audit.log) map cleanly onto the taxonomy, covering 6 of 7 categories directly and the 7th (multi-agent propagation) via a cited formal attack class rather than an observed-in-the-wild Agent Skills incident. The paper's discussion section concludes that only T6 (Persistence) has a 'favorable feasibility profile' for mitigation without specification changes or usability tradeoffs; direct prompt injection (T3.1) is judged an open problem with no complete defense achievable within the current architecture because malicious SKILL.md content already occupies the 'operator' trust layer, making instruction-hierarchy defenses like StruQ inapplicable.
core_contribution: The paper presents what it claims is the first systematic security analysis of Anthropic's Agent Skills framework (a filesystem-based, natural-language capability-packaging format for LLM agents, introduced Oct 2025 and since adopted by Cursor, GitHub Copilot, and Gemini CLI). It is explicitly labeled a 'Vision Paper' (CCS concept tag) with no new experiments, datasets, or attacks implemented by the authors. Its own contributions are: (1) a four-phase lifecycle decomposition of Agent Skills -- Creation, Distribution, Deployment, Execution -- with the structural attack surface each phase introduces; (2) a threat taxonomy of 7 categories and 17 named scenarios (T1-T7) organized into 3 attack layers (Delivery & Trust, Runtime Attack, Persistent & Lateral), constructed via a stated 3-stage process (literature/CVE review, first-principles structural analysis of the architecture, reconciliation of the two); (3) a mapping of 5 real-world security incidents onto the taxonomy as a coverage/validation argument; (4) a per-category discussion of defense directions and 7 open research challenges (C1-C7). A comparison table (Table 1) contrasts Agent Skills against ChatGPT Plugins and MCP across 8 security-relevant dimensions (data/instruction boundary, execution locus, runtime isolation, permission scope, trust model, marketplace review, authorship complexity), arguing Agent Skills is critically exposed ('✘✘') on the dimensions ChatGPT Plugins mitigates.
threat_model: Not an empirical attacker-capability threat model with formal adversary assumptions -- this is an architectural/structural threat taxonomy. The paper frames the core structural risk as: (a) absence of a data-to-instruction boundary in SKILL.md (frontmatter description vs. instructions body vs. runtime data are all processed identically as natural language by the LLM with no formal interface contract); (b) a single-approval persistent trust model in which installing a Skill grants it undifferentiated, persistent, content-version-independent operator-level authority over the agent's full action space A (filesystem, network, subprocess) with no per-action or per-session re-confirmation; (c) an open marketplace distribution model with no mandatory security review and near-zero authorship barrier (natural-language authorship needs no programming expertise); (d) bundled script execution where 'the script's source code never enters the context window -- only the script's output is returned to the agent,' so the LLM cannot inspect script behavior before acting on its results. The 7 threat categories are: T1 Supply Chain Compromise (typosquatting, ranking manipulation, repository hijacking, hallucinated packages), T2 Consent Abuse (consent gap, post-installation modification), T3 Prompt Injection (direct/indirect), T4 Code Execution (malicious script, deferred dependency, remote code fetch), T5 Data Exfiltration (credential harvesting, env-var harvesting, codebase exfiltration), T6 Persistence (memory-file poisoning e.g. AGENTS.md/MEMORY.md/SOUL.md, config injection e.g. settings.json/mcp.json), T7 Multi-Agent Propagation (prompt infection across orchestrator/subagent pipelines, citing Lee and Tiwari's formalization of self-replicating adversarial instructions).
limitations: The paper is explicit about its own status as a 'Vision Paper' with no original empirical measurement: all quantitative figures (42,447 Skills scanned / 26.1% vulnerable; 1,184 Skills compromised; 60+ cryptocurrency wallet categories harvested) are drawn from cited third-party industry/vendor research (Liu et al.; Koi Security; Antiy CERT; Authmind; Mitiga; SafeDep; Snyk; Cisco AI Defense; Check Point Research), several of which are vendor blog posts/threat-research writeups rather than peer-reviewed sources -- the authors do not independently verify these figures. The taxonomy's 'completeness' claim is explicitly hedged as coverage 'with respect to the current threat landscape as documented in public sources,' not a formal completeness proof. Defense-direction discussion is qualitative and non-prototyped -- no defense proposed in Section 7.1 was implemented or evaluated by the authors. The paper also carries visible artifacts suggesting it may be a template/placeholder submission rather than a finished, camera-ready piece: it cites a future J. ACM Vol. 37 No. 4 (August 2026) publication with placeholder DOI 'XXXXXXX.XXXXXXX,' and the arXiv identifier extracted from the source (2604.02837, dated 3 Apr 2026 in the PDF's own footer) does not correspond to a valid arXiv YYMM numbering scheme as of the paper's stated dates, which the reviewer flags as a grounding anomaly worth independent verification before this record is treated as fully authoritative bibliographic metadata.

quantitative_results: ClawHavoc campaign compromised over 1,184 Skills, approximately one in five packages in the ClawHub marketplace at time of discovery (cited from Koi Security / Antiy CERT, not authors' own measurement)

Liu et al.'s cited empirical study scanned 42,447 Skills and found 26.1% contained at least one security vulnerability spanning 14 distinct patterns across 4 categories (prompt injection, data exfiltration, privilege escalation, supply chain risks) -- cited, not reproduced by this paper
ClawHavoc malware harvested over 60 categories of cryptocurrency wallet data (cited from Koi Security/Antiy CERT)
CVE-2025-59536 CVSS score 8.7; CVE-2026-21852 CVSS score 5.3 (cited from Check Point Research), patched in Claude Code versions 1.0.111 and 2.0.65 respectively
Mitiga's demonstrated codebase exfiltration completed in 4 user interactions with an empty skill-audit.log (cited from Mitiga Labs)
This paper's own taxonomy: 7 threat categories, 17 threat scenarios, 3 attack layers, 4 lifecycle phases, 5 incidents analyzed, 7 open research challenges (C1-C7)
baselines_compared: ChatGPT Plugins (2023-2024) vs. Model Context Protocol (Nov 2024) vs. Agent Skills (Oct 2025), compared qualitatively across 8 dimensions in Table 1: data/instruction boundary, instruction carrier, execution locus, runtime isolation, permission scope, trust model, marketplace review, authorship complexity -- this is an architectural/qualitative comparison, not an empirical benchmark with baselines and metrics
